coredata.dc:title: Gardeniae Fructus derived natural small molecule-based carbon dots promoting activation and aggregation of platelets to accelerate hemostasis

coredata.dc:description: Effective and prompt hemostasis poses a formidable challenge currently, especially for tissue injury-induced endogenous hemorrhage. Carbonic herbs originated from traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) have been historically utilized in the treatment of endogenous hemorrhages, but the underlying active ingredients and hemostatic mechanism still remain largely unexplored. Study on carbon dots (CDs) prepared by main small molecule components from TCM is of great significance for unravelling the mechanism of action of carbonic herbs. Gardeniae Fructus is a classical TCM which widely used for hemostasis after carbonization. Given that geniposide is one of main small molecule components of it, geniposide-carbon dots (Gen-CDs) are developed using modified hydrothermal method and considered as the key pharmacological agents responsible for the hemostatic efficacy of carbonized Gardeniae Fructus (CGF). Undergoing repeated dehydration and condensation, Gen-CDs having abundant functional groups were obtained from geniposide, exhibiting superior water solubility and bioavailability. The mouse liver injury model, tail amputation model, and rat blood-heat hemorrhage (BHH) syndrome model were all employed to confirm the excellent hemostatic activity of Gen-CDs on exogenous hemorrhage. The in vitro and vivo experiments had indicated that Gen-CDs could effectively promote platelet activation and aggregation. Overall, the successful preparation of Gen-CDs with effective and prompt hemostatic performance could provide a new perspective for elucidating the substantial basis and mechanism of carbonic herbs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonic herbs
Carbonized Gardeniae Fructus (CGF)
Geniposide-carbon dots (Gen-CDs)
Hemostasis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the CGF and Geniposide derived CDs
Preparation and characterizations of CGF-CDs and Gen-CDs
Transformation mechanism from Gen into Gen-CDs
Hemostatic properties in vitro and vivo of Gen-CDs
Hemostatic effects of Gen-CDs on blood-heat hemorrhage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographic images of CGF-CDs and Gen-CDs aqueous solutions under daylight and 365 nm UV illumination. (b) TEM image of CGF-CDs. Scale bar: 20 nm. (c) HRTEM image of CGF-CDs. Scale bar: 5 nm. (d) TEM image of Gen-CDs. Scale bar: 20 nm. (e) HRTEM image of Gen-CDs. Scale bar: 5 nm. (f) The size distribution of CFG-CDs and Gen-CDs from TEM image. (g) FT-IR spectra of CGF-CDs and Gen-CDs. (h) Fluorescence emission spectra of CGF-CDs and Gen-CDs. (i) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of Gen-CDs. (j) Fluorescence emission spectra of Gen-CDs with excitation wavelengths of 320–420 nm. (k) Raman diffraction spectrum of Gen-CDs.

(a) XPS spectrum of Gen-CDs and geniposide: Full survey spectrum, (b) C 1 s, and (c) O 1 s. (d) FT-IR spectra of Gen-CDs and geniposide. (e) TGA and DTGs for geniposide, and (f) Gen-CDs. (g) UV–vis absorption of Gen-CDs and geniposide. (h) 1H NMR spectra of Gen-CDs and geniposide. (i) Zeta potential of Gen-CDs and geniposide.
Derivation of possible formation mechanism of Gen-CDs.

(a) SEM of the blood clot in the blank control, YNBY (300 μg·mL−1), and Gen-CDs (150 and 300 μg·mL−1) administration, respectively. Scale bar: 10 μm. (b) Schematic diagram of the trauma model of mice tail cutting and liver laceration. (c) Histogram of bleeding time in tail cutting experiment. (d) Histogram of bleeding time in liver laceration experiment. SD rats were assigned into five groups (n = 6). Effects of Gen-CDs on coagulation parameters. (e) t-PA, (f) PAI-1, (g) TXB2, (h) 6-keto-PGF1α, (i) Platelet aggregation rate, and (j) PLT. “NC” means black control group, “HC” means Hemocoagulase group, “H”, “M” and “L” means Gen-CDs at high- (12.00 mg·kg−1), medium- (6.00 mg·kg−1) and low (3.00 mg·kg−1) doses. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001; #p < 0.05, ##p < 0.01, ###p < 0.001).

(a) Macroscopic images of acute gastric mucosal injury. (b) H&E staining of the gastric of BHH syndrome rats on day 8. Scale bar: 100 μm. Blood harvested on day 8 was analyzed for (c) APTT, (d) PT, (e)TT, (f) FIB (n = 6). “NC” means negative control group, “YNBY” means Yunnan Baiyao group, “GCH” and “GCL” means Gen-CDs at high (12.00 mg·kg−1) and low (6.00 mg·kg−1) doses. (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001.).

(a) Incubation of platelets with different concentrations of Gen-CDs to detect intracellular calcium ions. Scale bar: 50 μm. (b) Fluorescent signal map of FITC-labeled Rat⁃CD62P antibody flow-through.

(a) Direct view of the hemolysis experiment in vitro. (b) Histogram of the hemolysis rates in vitro. (c) Cell viability of primary cardiomyocytes treated with different concentrations of Gen-CDs. (d) Weight changes between control and Gen-CDs administration groups within 14 days. (e) The changes of ALT, AST and CRE levels between control and Gen-CDs administration groups. (f) Organ index of mice between control and Gen-CDs administration groups. (g) H&E staining of lung, liver, spleen, heart and kidney. Scale bar: 100 μm.
Schematic illustration of preparation process of Gen-CDs.

head.ce:title: Gardeniae Fructus derived natural small molecule-based carbon dots promoting activation and aggregation of platelets to accelerate hemostasis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective and prompt hemostasis poses a formidable challenge currently, especially for tissue injury-induced endogenous hemorrhage. Carbonic herbs originated from traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) have been historically utilized in the treatment of endogenous hemorrhages, but the underlying active ingredients and hemostatic mechanism still remain largely unexplored. Study on carbon dots (CDs) prepared by main small molecule components from TCM is of great significance for unravelling the mechanism of action of carbonic herbs. Gardeniae Fructus is a classical TCM which widely used for hemostasis after carbonization. Given that geniposide is one of main small molecule components of it, geniposide-carbon dots (Gen-CDs) are developed using modified hydrothermal method and considered as the key pharmacological agents responsible for the hemostatic efficacy of carbonized Gardeniae Fructus (CGF). Undergoing repeated dehydration and condensation, Gen-CDs having abundant functional groups were obtained from geniposide, exhibiting superior water solubility and bioavailability. The mouse liver injury model, tail amputation model, and rat blood-heat hemorrhage (BHH) syndrome model were all employed to confirm the excellent hemostatic activity of Gen-CDs on exogenous hemorrhage. The in vitro and vivo experiments had indicated that Gen-CDs could effectively promote platelet activation and aggregation. Overall, the successful preparation of Gen-CDs with effective and prompt hemostatic performance could provide a new perspective for elucidating the substantial basis and mechanism of carbonic herbs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Effective hemostasis plays a vital role in life-saving interventions during warfare, surgical procedures, congenital disorders, and medical treatments. Rapid and efficient control of bleeding is key to dramatically reducing mortality and saving lives [1]. However, existing hemostatic materials such as zeolite, chitosan, hemostatic powders and gauzes, often cause local burns and wound infections, and struggle to penetrate deeply into tissues and organs. Additionally, some coagulation proteins used for treating internal bleeding can cause severe side effects due to immune responses [2]. Therefore, there is a pressing demand for the development of new hemostatic strategies for endogenous hemorrhage.

Carbonic herbs, integral to TCM, are well-known for remarkable hemostatic effect and high biosafety. According to a unique theoretical framework of traditional Chinese medical science, they have been used for treating various hemorrhagic diseases since the Han Dynasty [3]. After calcining in sealed container or stir-frying in high heat, carbonic herbs are obtained when appears as a burnt black color. This process is termed as carbonization. Thereafter, chemical ingredients in TCM precursor have been significantly altered resulting in the change of the characteristics and pharmacological properties. On account of complex and diverse components like polysaccharide, inorganic substances and small molecules in TCM, elucidating the pharmacodynamic material basis has become a formidable challenge for popularization and application of carbonic herbs [4,5].

In recent years, accumulating studies have shown that remarkable pharmacological performance of carbonic herbs might be attributed to CDs, which is a novel type of carbon nanomaterial with a particle size smaller than 10 nm [6]. Varied of substances can be prepared as CDs, which usually applied for drug delivery, bio-imaging, DNA condensation, and treatment for disease due to their high cell permeability, water solubility and low toxicity [7]. However, the formation mechanism and intrinsic bioactivity of CDs are still the blind spot in research. It was reported that various CDs with excellent hemostatic capabilities had been successfully prepared and isolated from diverse carbonic herbs, such as Phellodendri cortex Carbonisata [8], Pollen typhae Carbonisata [9], and Junci medulla Carbonisata [10]. But as crucial active ingredients, CDs based on natural small molecules (NSMs) from TCM precursors have not attracted sufficient attention. The exact transformation process from NSMs to CDs and mechanism of action for hemostasis in carbonized TCM remains indeterminate.

Gardeniae Fructus (GF) is a commonly used TCM in the clinical, which characterized by its chilly nature, bitter taste, and multifaceted pharmacological effects, including liver protection, relief of swelling and pain, cooling blood and detoxification, et al. [11]. Numerous chemical compositions had been isolated and identified from GF, wherein geniposide was one of main NSM components accounting over 6 % [12]. CGF is a traditional carbonic herb originated from GF, which have been predominantly utilized in treating hemoptysis, blood vomiting, and coughing up blood. In view of high similarity in the carbonization process, the CDs formed by geniposide under high-temperature and low-oxygen conditions might take considerable responsibility for hemostatic effect of CGF.

In this study, we prepared the Gen-CDs successfully through modified hydrothermal method aiming to simulate the carbonic process of GF ( Scheme 1). By a series of spectral techniques like ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis), Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR), and fluorescence spectrum, Gen-CDs were identified as the primary material form in CGF. Subsequently, we elucidated the formation mechanism of Gen-CDs by comprehensive characterization of their morphology, size, crystallinity, and optical properties, along with an analysis of surface functional groups and elemental composition. The hemostasis effect of Gen-CDs was demonstrated using the mouse liver damage model, tail amputation model, and rat BHH syndrome model. It was found that Gen-CDs could effectively shorten clotting time and augment coagulation through increasing platelet count, restraining the fibrinolytic system, promoting the release of platelet activating factor, and elevating intracellular calcium ion concentration in platelets. Additionally, the good biosafety of Gen-CDs was also verified. Collectively, the development of Gen-CDs is of prime significance to unravelling the material basis of CGF, and also provide a novel NSM-based candidate with highly hemostatic function originated from traditional carbonic herbs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the CGF and Geniposide derived CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Two 30-min boils were achieved by soaking 10 g of CGF powder in 100 mL of deionized water. To purify the CGF-CDs, the resulting solution was concentrated, filtered to remove residue, and dialyzed for 72 h with a 14 kDa membrane. Geniposide powder (1.0 g) was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water before being placed in a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for three hours. The obtained mixture was centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 r/min. The brown-yellow clarified solution was then pretreated with a 0.22 μm membrane, and the mixture was dialyzed for 72 h using a 1 kDa dialysis membrane to purify the Gen-CDs. In the end, Gen-CDs’ powder was obtained after 2 days of freeze-drying (1 g of raw material can yield 200 mg of Gen-CDs).
The details of materials, characterization, hemostatic properties and biocompatibility in vitro and vivo, and hemostatic mechanism analysis were provided in the Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of CGF-CDs and Gen-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In general, most of the preparation methods of CDs require a high temperature over 200 ℃. The optimum temperature for frying GF into CGF is 180 ℃. In order to simulate the processing environment for carbonic herbs, we employed modified hydrothermal approach at 180 ℃ to yield Gen-CDs. In daylight, the two CDs solution were both light brownish yellow. While exposed to UV light at a wavelength of 365 nm, similar distinct blue photoluminescence (PL) of them could be clearly observed ( Fig. 1a). Initially, the morphology of the CGF-CDs and Gen-CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). We could observe that they were both almost spherical in shape with nearly the same average diameter (≈3 nm) with non-uniform lattice fringes (d100 = 0.21–0.22 nm) [13] between adjacent locations Fig. 1b-f. Furthermore, the comparative analysis by FT-IR and fluorescence spectroscopy suggested that the functional groups and core structures of Gen-CDs was substantially identical with CGF-CDs (Fig. 1g, h). Given that geniposide was one of the main components of GF, it was reasonable to believe Gen-CDs might play a key role in CGF. Thus, we had regarded Gen-CDs as the major object in a subsequent study. There is a strong emission at approximately 428 nm and a strong excitation at 360 nm in PL spectrum of Gen-CDs aqueous solutions (Fig. 1i). The emission wavelength redshifted from 422 to 492 nm when excitation wavelength increased in PL excitation spectrum (Fig. 1j). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of Gen-CDs showed a broad reflection peak at 20.6 °, suggesting that graphitic carbons with disordered carbon atoms exhibited (002) diffraction facets (Fig. S1) [14]. In Raman spectrum (Fig. 1k), we could find two classical signal bands, D band (disordered, sp3) and G band (graphitized, sp2) at approximately 1350 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. The intensity ratio (ID/IG) of Gen-CDs was 0.87, indicating that the Gen-CDs had high carbon lattice-structure content [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transformation mechanism from Gen into Gen-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: At present, formation mechanism of CDs is still the difficult point for researchers, resulting in limited application. In terms of Gen-CDs, we had investigated their formation mechanism deeply by various technologies. In X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of geniposide and Gen-CDs, only two stack plots at 285 and 532 eV for C1s and O1s ( Fig. 2a). In comparison to geniposide, the percentage of carbon elements in Gen-CDs increased by 8 % (Table S1), indicating a higher carbonization level. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) confirmed the presence of C-C (284.7 eV), C-O (286.3 eV), CO (287.8 eV), and O-CO (288.6 eV). The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c) at 532.5 eV and 531.5 eV showed that Gen-CDs contained C-O and CO [16]. As determined by both C1s and O1s spectrum certified data in Table S1, C-O percentage in Gen-CDs (20.68 %) was significantly lower than in geniposide (33.3 %), possibly due to the dehydration and polymerization processes. Moreover, Gen-CDs had a lower CO ratio of 12.01 % compared to gardenoside. The characteristic absorption peaks at 3405 cm−1, 1710 cm−1, and 1035 cm−1 were found in FT-IR spectrum of geniposide and Gen-CDs (Fig. 2d), which were suggestive of O-H, CO, and C-O bonds [17]. Geniposide could be dehydrated and polymerized during carbonization, and some oxygen-containing functional groups could decompose at high temperatures.

Next, the thermal stability and surface compositions of geniposide and Gen-CDs were explored through Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). Derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) indicated that geniposide had three decomposition stages (Fig. 2e). However, the DTG measurement of Gen-CDs exhibited four decomposition stages (Fig. 2f). As a result of more decomposition stages than geniposide, Gen-CDs exhibited a greater degree of thermal stability. The first stage (30–143.2 °C) showed a slight mass loss caused by the evaporation of water molecules adsorbed on geniposide and Gen-CDs. Compared to geniposide after 143.2 °C, Gen-CDs had a difference weight loss primarily due to the changes in the process of decomposition of oxygen-containing functional groups. A lower mass loss of 15.7 % in Gen-CDs at the second stage (143.2–424.2 °C) than geniposide reveals a lower content of CO and C-O-C, which is consistent with a lower FT-IR peak intensity ratio of VC-O-C to VC
O of Gen-CDs (Fig. 2d). The last stage between 424.2 and 570.9 °C shows a higher mass loss of 2.78 % in Gen-CDs than geniposide due to the decomposition of sp2 and sp3 carbons demonstrated by FT-IR and XPS analyses [18,19]. After 570.9 °C, the residue with a constant mass percentage was hard to decompose. Then, in UV–vis adsorption spectroscopy (Fig. 2g), a shoulder peak at 280 nm attributed to
π -
π
*
electron transition of sp2 domains of carbon atoms was observed in Gen-CDs, which may have resulted in higher degrees of carbonization [20]. The maximum absorption peaks at 240 nm of Gen-CDs and geniposide were attributed to the presence of
α ,
β
-unsaturated carbonyl structures [21]. Fig. 2h showed the major changes in 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) with dotted lines. Clearly, the number of hydrogen proton signals in Gen-CDs increased between 4.0 and 8.0 ppm (Fig. S2, and Fig. S3). Compared to gardeniside, the newly added hydrogen proton signals at 4.0–6.0 ppm indicated hydroxyl groups on the benzene ring, while the newly appeared hydrogen proton signals at 6.0–8.0 ppm might correspond to the H on the benzene ring. Therefore, Gen-CDs were found to have more aromatic structural domains than geniposide, which was in accordance with 13C NMR results (Fig. S4). Moreover, the zeta potential for Gen-CDs in water was determined as −24 mV (Fig. 2i), implying that there was an abundance of active groups on the surface. The zeta potential for the same concentration geniposide in water is −13 mV. Accordingly, the stable Gen-CDs could be prepared successfully via the modified hydrothermal method.

In conclusion, Gen-CDs were prepared by hydrothermal method with an abundance of functional groups, resulting in high water solubility and stability. Based on the above analysis, we speculated the creation of Gen-CDs involves a complicated chemical route ( Fig. 3). Considering all these factors, it is plausible that the formation of Gen-CDs is primarily the result of glucose being dehydrated in geniposide to form products 1–3. This route led to the formation of 4 which can account for the FT-IR bands of VC
O. These species could condense through aldol process and dehydration forming species such as 5 and 6. Moreover, this route forms compounds like 7, which can readily result in the creation of aromatic cores with limited functionality. At the outer edges of this structure, both dehydration and aldol reactions cause it to continue to expand. The jump absorption peak at 280 nm in the UV spectrum, the increase in aromatic hydrogens in the 1H NMR spectrum, and the increase in CC and CO content in the XPS spectrum demonstrated that Gen-CDs were rich in aromatic structural domains and oxygen-containing functional groups at the structural edges.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemostatic properties in vitro and vivo of Gen-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As a classical carbonic herb, CGF have been utilized widely in clinical practice to treat hemorrhagic disorders under guide of TCM theory [22]. In order to explore whether Gen-CDs derived from geniposide might be one of the representative active ingredients of CGF, we evaluated the hemostatic effects of them thoroughly. Red blood cells are crucial for thrombosis or clot formation. Hence, the in vitro erythrocyte adhesion function of Gen-CDs at different concentrations was tested by SEM, and YNBY was used as the positive control group. As shown in Fig. 4a, Gen-CDs with high concentration could form more extensive and dense fibrin networks than YNBY, while there was little evidence of fibrin network in the normal control group, suggesting that Gen-CDs could efficiently promote erythrocyte aggregation for blood coagulation. Next, the hemostatic effect in vivo of Gen-CDs was also assessed using mouse tail amputation and liver scratch models (Fig. 4b). Hemocoagulase (HC) was used as positive control group. In the two models, the bleeding time and amount of blood loss in the low dose of Gen-CDs group was comparable to that of HC group, which also both decreased significantly with the increasing of concentration of Gen-CDs (Fig. 4c, d and S5). It was demonstrated preliminarily that Gen-CDs exhibited rapid and remarkable hemostatic activity.

Moreover, t-PA and PAI-1 are two markers of steady state of fibrinolytic system, which can affect hemostasis by acting on the formation of thrombus and clotting [23]. It was showed that Gen-CDs could inhibit the fibrinolytic system to elicit hemostatic effects by decreasing t-PA and increasing PAI-1 levels in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 4e, f). Furthermore, a series of tests of routine blood and blood coagulation factors tests were conducted in normal rats after the treatment of Gen-CDs. By testing, we could find that TXB2 and 6-keto-PGF1α production in each dosage group of Gen-CDs were strikingly higher than those of normal and HC groups, and the action depended on their doses (Fig. 4g, h). As such, it was suggested that Gen-CDs could promote platelets activation and aggregation through inducing the synthesis of the procoagulant factor TXA2 and inhibiting the production of the anticoagulant factor PGI2, which was most likely correlated with the negatively charged surface of Gen-CDs [2]. Compared with the blank and HC groups, the platelet aggregation rate after Gen-CDs administration was significantly increased (Fig. 4i), which further verified the role of Gen-CDs in promoting coagulation. As displayed in Fig. 4j, all doses of Gen-CDs and HC could increase PLT count dramatically. Based on the significant results, it appeared that Gen-CDs primarily affected platelet activation and aggregation, as well as increasing platelet counts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemostatic effects of Gen-CDs on blood-heat hemorrhage
ce:section.ce:para: As a common bleeding disease, BHH syndrome may cause serious endogenous tissue damage. It has been verified that CGF can provide considerable clinical benefit to relieve BBH syndrome for many years. To further appraise the hemostatic efficacy of Gen-CDs on endogenous hemorrhage, we utilized subcutaneous injection of yeast plus intragastrical administration of alcohol to establish the rat model with BHH syndrome. YNBY was selected as the positive group. We could observe that there was no significant difference in body weights among all groups (Fig. S6), suggesting that Gen-CDs would not induce systemic toxicity. The rats were subcutaneously injected with 20 % dry yeast suspension at one hour after the last treatment. As reported in the literature, the rectal temperature of rats began to rise after injection of yeast for 3 h and maintained at a constant high level during 5–7 h (Fig. S7). After that, the rats were orally administrated with high concentrations of ethanol, which could cause acute gastric mucosal lesions such as mucosal edema, congestion of the glandular region, and linear hemorrhagic necrosis due to accumulation of heat produced by the combination of dry yeast and ethanol [24].

As presented in Fig 5a, stomach in NC group exhibited normal morphology, while there were severe hemorrhagic lesions in the gastric mucosa on Model group. By contrast, these symptoms were alleviated apparently in the treatment groups. In order to assess the gastroprotective and hemostatic effect of Gen-CDs, the ulcer area and percentage inhibition were quantified using ImageJ software. The ethanol-induced acute gastric ulcer model was successful in maintaining its large Ulcer index (UI) (45.33 ± 8.41 %) (Fig. S8a). A significant decrease in the UI was observed in all rats of the GCL (21.35 ± 4.35 %), GCH (7.81 ± 2.58 %), and YNBY (20.73 ± 4.58 %) groups (p < 0.001). Additionally, compared to the model group, the percentage inhibition (Fig. S8b) of the YNBY, GCL, and GCH groups increased significantly (p < 0.01). From Fig. 5b, the histopathological features of microscopic alterations in stomach tissues were further explored by H&E staining. The gastric mucosa of rats in the Model group both appeared bleeding and the presence of local inflammatory cells. Exhilaratingly, different doses of Gen-CDs and YNBY could significantly relieve the symptoms such as mucosal cells scattered hemorrhages, edema, and inflammatory cell infiltration.

Next, various coagulation parameters of rat plasma in all groups were examined including activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), prothrombin time (PT), thrombin time (TT) and fibrinogen (FIB), which are often used to evaluate the efficacy of coagulation function. Compared with Model group (17.87 ± 0.80 s), the APTT were notably decreased in GCH group (15.83 ± 0.55 s), which were suggestive of reduced bleeding tendency (Fig. 5c). GCL and GCH group also showed a conspicuous decrease in PT values (11.35 ± 0.38 s and 10.81 ± 0.57 s, respectively) in comparison with that of Model group (12.33 ± 0.52 s) (Fig. 5d). As previously reported, APTT and PT values were closely related to the endogenous and exogenous coagulation pathway, respectively [25]. Meanwhile, the TT value after GCH treatment (24.43 ± 0.87 s) was lower than that of the Model group (28.28 ± 0.69 s) (Fig. 5e). Theoretically, TT value can reflect the activity of FIB conversion to fibrin in plasma, which is a major protein involved in the clotting process. In accordance with the results of TT, FIB value would increase significantly after GCH treatment compared to the Model group (Fig. 5f). Besides, the blood routine of rats in all groups was detected. Notably, compared with Model group (1040 ± 120 × 109/L), the platelets in GCL and GCH groups were drastically increased to (1274 ± 111) × 109/L and (1250 ± 55.34) × 109/L, respectively (Fig. S9). From these results, we could find that the efficacy of Gen-CDs with high dose was equivalent with positive drug, YNBY, which was a well-known TCM formula exhibiting exceptional hemostatic effects. However, the active ingredients of it have not been disclosed on account of the state secret principle. As CDs transformed by definite NSMs, the source of Gen-CDs was different from YNBY essentially. Therefore, Gen-CDs has the potential to become hemostatic drug candidates widely used in clinical practice.

The concentration of intracellular calcium ions would be increased when platelets are activated [26,27]. As a commonly used fluorescent probe for detecting intracellular calcium ion, Fluo-3 AM is capable of binding with calcium ions to yield strong fluorescence [28]. There was almost no fluorescence of PBS group in Fig. 6a. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity increased with the rise of dose of Gen-CDs, suggesting that they could promote platelet activation. Furthermore, the effect of Gen-CDs on platelet activation rate was also assessed via detecting the fluorescence intensity on the platelet surface using flow cytometry. P-selectin (CD62P) was used as an activation-specific marker for platelets [29]. In resting platelets, CD62P can be found in the alpha granules, which are released during platelet adhesion and aggregation [30]. Compared to the PBS group, Gen-CDs could significantly increase the expression of CD62P. When the concentration of Gen-CDs reached 37.5 μg/mL, CD62P expression was 40.18 % (Fig. 6b). Therefore, it was concluded that Gen-CDs exhibited synergistic promotion of platelet activation.

Given that the biosafety was a fundamental factor for clinical application of CDs [31], we also evaluated safety of Gen-CDs in vitro and vivo. In hemolytic test in vitro, we could find that erythrocyte membranes in blank group were ruptured, resulting in the overflow of hemoglobin. Nevertheless, the erythrocytes after Gen-CDs treatment sank to the bottom, whose hemolysis rate was much less than the national standard (5 %) ( Fig. 7a, b). In addition, the cytotoxicity of Gen-CDs on primary myocardial cells was appraised by MTT. As can be seen in Fig. 7c, Gen-CDs had almost no influence to the survival of cells even at 300 μg·mL−1. Next, we investigated their biosafety in vivo utilizing healthy mice. After intraperitoneal injection of PBS (control group) and Gen-CDs (150 mg/kg) for 14 days, there was no significant difference in body weights in both groups (Fig. 7d), indicating that Gen-CDs could not induce the systemic toxicity. Through detecting the levels of alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), and creatinine (CRE) in the serum, it was demonstrated that Gen-CDs would not affect liver and renal function (Fig. 7e). After euthanizing the mice, the major organs including lung, liver, spleen, heart, and kidney were obtained. The organ index of mice in Gen-CDs group had no changes compared to those in blank group (Fig. 7f). A comparison of macroscopic images of the lung, liver, spleen, heart, and kidney did not reveal any significant differences between the two groups (Fig. S10). Further histopathologic analysis of them showed no inflammatory cell infiltration or any other abnormal damage after Gen-CDs treatment (Fig. 7g). Due to the natural source of Gen-CDs, they had superior biosafety comparing with other synthetic CDs materials. Therefore, we think it could be very suitable for the clinical application.

In this study, the novel Gen-CDs originated from Gen were developed using modified hydrothermal method for hemostasis inspired by preparation process of classical carbonic herb, GCF. Then, we delved into the transformation mechanism from Gen into Gen-CDs. It was speculated that a series of chemical reactions including dehydration, aldol reaction, and aromatization led to the formation of Gen-CDs. The results of experiments in vitro and vivo showed that Gen-CDs had excellent hemostatic properties. According to the results of a mouse liver injury model and a tail amputation model, Gen-CDs can significantly reduce the time required for hemostasis. BHH syndrome in rats demonstrated that Gen-CDs relieved gastric mucosal bleeding, increased platelet counts, and promoted coagulation. Combining the results of coagulation factors and tests, Gen-CDs was mainly accomplished by activating common coagulation pathway, increasing TXB2 content in plasma and promoting platelet aggregation. Further investigation into the impact of Gen-CDs on platelets revealed that they could significantly increase the concentration of platelet intracellular calcium ions and the expression of CD62P. Therefore, we find the excellent hemostatic activity of Gen-CDs was mostly related to promote platelet activation and aggregation. In addition, Gen-CDs were more stable than HC and had a simpler composition than YNBY, which benefited to reduce immune response and control the efficacy quality. Overall, our findings suggested that Gen-CDs could become promising candidates for treating bleeding disorders and provided a new perspective for elucidating the substantial basis and mechanism of carbonic herbs.

Xiaowei Huo: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Data curation. Xudong Xu: Resources. Guoxu Ma: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yong Hou: Writing – original draft, Data curation. Jiawen Zhang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Yihang Li: Conceptualization. Xueshan Xu: Methodology, Investigation. Zhaocui Sun: Visualization. Zhonghao Sun: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was financially supported by the CAMS Innovation Fund for Medical Sciences (CIFMS) (2021-I2M-1-071), the Natural Science Foundation of Henan (232300420221), and the Yunnan Key Laboratory of Southern Medicine Utilization (2024-YZS-01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2025.114601.
